column12: Is there anything you would like to tell us about your experience?
I find it hard to upload documents as the drop down menu times out quicker than you can click on it.

Drawing a diagram of the site was necessarily challenging. I do a lot of IT work and it was difficult for me.  I do not think this will be possible for some people particularly those who did not have IT skills or older.  I don’t want to be overcritical as I know it’s a difficult thing to design, but I think it does need improvement.
Its seem straight forward uploading and completing my application.
Difficult to navigate.
As a normal householder it is really hard to work out what category you should be using for certain submissions.

This is very confusing. When the Planning Portal service people amended the plan (why we could not delete the interior shapes, I don't understand), I thought I had submitted it again, but obviously I didn't because the green application bar did not show 100% even though all the elements were completed. Please make this simpler for the older generation like us.
Very good

It seems unnecessary to provide a description of the uploaded documents when we are also selecting the type of document.  Also, sometimes it isn't clear which documents are outstanding and need to be uploaded; it would help if they were listed at the top of the upload page or highlighted in RED.  Otherwise, it is straightforward to use.  Thank you.
Service is amazing once you familiarise yourself with its application
Not enough options for change of use applications

Excellent

little unwieldy.

Hopefully now I’ve applied once via the portal, next time should become easier!  I also think a drawn sketch isn’t needed after uploading such informative photos from Google Maps

Absolutely terrible. There are clearly many bugs in this web site. In particular on trying to submit an application for an LDC there was no information as to which option should be selected to ensure that one half of the appropriate fee would apply and no way to identify a request for the disability exemption. Also the system will not allow you to proceed unless you have fully completed the application which is understandable but the system does not tell you precisely what remains outstanding so you have to spend hours literally trying to find out. To say application is 82%  complete doesn't help. I telephoned the help line about this and the gentlemen I spoke to was most unhelpful stating that I hadn't completed the application  which is why it would not allow me to proceed - obviously I knew that already. I could go on Because of all these glitches in the system I estimate that I have had to spend approximately 2 days !! trying to work out how to finalise the application. I don't suppose any one will take any notice of this BUT it clearly needs urgent attention.  I do note also that the application changed several times whilst I was attempting to negotiate it-  for example it would say  2 documents had been upload and then that only one had been up loaded so you have to repeat the whole process again. Also after I spoke to your office it changed after that conversation as the pages about the exemption suddenly appeared although not present before that conversation took place
no
I think it was ok. I hope I have managed to submit all i was asked to do.
change file size pls ...some LPAs still on 10mb only which is ridiculous.

You could make the uploading of documents a lot easier.  The tick list is in an illogical order, some headings seem unnecessary, some seem to be duplications, and there are specific other documents that should be listed.

I used the Portal to submit my Planning Application, after initial consultation. I am hoping that with the assistance of the consultation guidance notes, my application will be successful. I feel the £72 Consultation plus a further £91 total £163, against application cost of £548 is on the higher percentage, and would have thought having paid consultation the service fee would be reduced.
hopeless, even your staff told me it was

Yes - the system would not allow my 8 files to upload despite me trying twice. The system only allows for 6 files maybe because of the max capacity. This was very inconvenient and frustrating as the information required the full 8 files as proof of the carbon emission calculations. Also last time I did a discharge application I paid £400 with a uk debit card denominated in sterling only to be charged in Euros. This was crazy and cost me another £30. Please refund my £30 soon. It did ask for my location at the time which was Italy which was correct as I’m on holiday but this was not relevant as I was paying with a uk bank card in £ sterling. Please respond to me about these 2 issues. Many thanks Michael Brereton 10.7.26.
easy of use and information available to help with application
Very straightforward.
I am fairly new to this and have a two Building Control and one Planning Application in, the system is not responsive, so pretty useless really. Thank you.

Don't get me wrong - its a great idea! But, I wish you mentioned at the start of the application process that a location plan was included in the fee! I had already purchased one from a separate website by the time I realised.  Apart from that it was all great. Application process seems to get easier every time I use it!

Revising/setting boundary is a nightmare when the auto function puts red lines around the buildings on the site, and there’s no way to delete them. Hand drawing not easy on irregular sites. How does someone not computer-literate manage?
more easy access to help and advice would be good. Not all the requirements are readily comprehensible

no thank you

This was a brilliant UX experience.  It made a potentially complicated process really easy.  I love the help notes during each step.  Clearly somebody has put a lot of work into the design of this portal.

Many application forms contain a large number of questions that are not relevant to the majority of proposals. This applies not only to householder applications but also to many full planning applications, such as change of use schemes, where applicants are still required to work through numerous sections that do not apply to the development.  It would be much more efficient if the application process included an initial screening or overview page where applicants could simply tick which topics are not applicable to their proposal. The system could then automatically hide or skip those sections, rather than requiring users to navigate through multiple pages and answer numerous "No" or "Not Applicable" questions.  This would make the process significantly quicker, reduce frustration, and help less experienced applicants and agents avoid spending unnecessary time trying to understand and complete irrelevant sections of the form.

I would like to provide feedback regarding the additional Planning Portal service charge.  As an applicant, I have completed the application form myself, prepared the supporting statement, uploaded all plans and supporting documents, and entered all required information directly into the system. Given that the applicant undertakes the majority of the administrative work, it is difficult to understand the justification for an additional service charge on top of the statutory planning fee.  The explanation that applications would otherwise need to be manually uploaded by the local authority is also difficult to reconcile with the fact that applicants are already providing all information in a digital format through the Planning Portal. From a customer perspective, the process appears to reduce rather than increase administrative work for both the applicant and the local authority.  Whilst I appreciate that there are costs associated with maintaining the Planning Portal system, greater transparency regarding how the service charge is calculated and the specific services it funds would be welcomed.  The Planning Portal provides a valuable service, but the additional charge can feel disproportionate, particularly for straightforward household applications where applicants have completed and submitted all documentation themselves.

My application was for a very simple matter, namely the like-for-like replacement of windows at the rear of the building. Yet, even for such a minor and straightforward matter, there were an enormous number of confusing and irrelevant questions on the application. I had to research answers for each. The whole process was very time consuming for what should have been a very straightforward matter. Then, after literally hours of trying to complete the form, I was blocked at the last hurdle when it came to paying due a technical issue (NOT on my side). But, by that time, your support team had gone home so I was blocked and could not get assistance to proceed to complete the application. It has been a very exasperating and dispiriting experience. You need to make the process simpler for simple applications such as mine. You need to find a way to cut out the irrelevant questions. Also, it was annoying how the CIL form had to be downloaded and completed totally separately. You should somehow incorporate this in the main application because that was another item blocking the completion of my application form and it took time for me to understand that it was a separate item to download and complete. Finally, I also feel that for a simple application such as mine, the fee is excessively high. So, overall, it has not been a good experience and one that has left me utterly drained and demoralised.  I have made the application in good faith because I want to do things properly and I feel you need to make the process simple and easy for those who follow the rules and are trying to do things properly. If you make it a difficult and complicated process and then apply high fees on top, you just put people off. It is very important that everyone follows the rules and do things properly but, you must make it easy and cost effective otherwise you encourage people to avoid doing so.

I submitted a claim for CLEUD.    1) Many questions are about "proposed" vs "existing" but in this case there is no proposed development, because all the changes took place 39 years ago! I had do some rather extreme logical gymnastics in order to guess/assume that (bizarrely) "proposed" in this case must actually mean "as currently existing for the past 39 years", and "existing" actually means "as it was more than 39 years ago before any change"  2) There was no structural work done, only a change of use, but the forms are not designed for this.   3) At the stage of adding documents, it said only 1 out 2 steps were complete, but there was no indication of what was missing so I was unable to proceed.  At first I guessed that probably I am required to submit a "Site Plan" even though it is virtually the same as the "location Plan"  so I used the RequestaPlan service to create a Site Plan and added it.   But it still said 1 out of 2 steps complete, and I was unable to proceed.  It seems that I had wasted nearly £10 at RequestaPlan.  My next guess was that perhaps I have to complete a CIL form.  (That seemed pointless really, as again this form is not designed for CLEUD cases where there is no development or structural work, only a change of use.) That did the trick.  But it should be clear - the software knows and imposes this CIL requirement.  So it should tell me!  I shouldn't have to guess .

I have used the Planning Portal for the past 20 years across a wide range of applications, and while it remains a widely recognised platform, there are several areas where the service could be improved.  The system itself is generally functional, but it can feel overly complex and not particularly user-friendly, especially for straightforward submissions. Navigation between sections is not always intuitive, and the process can be more time-consuming than necessary. In addition, the cost of submitting applications through the platform is relatively high, particularly when compared to the level of service provided.  Communication and transparency could also be improved. It is not always clear what stage an application is at without additional follow-up, and the overall user experience feels somewhat dated.  It is also worth noting that there are alternative platforms and submission routes available which offer a more streamlined experience and, in some cases, provide incentives or added value for using their services. This highlights that there is room for the Planning Portal to evolve and remain competitive.  Overall, while the Planning Portal serves its purpose, there is definite scope for improvement in terms of usability, value for money, and overall user experience.

Website application system is good and relatively straight forward to use. This is extremely positive. However, in terms of the actual planning system, I'm not sure that adequate consideration was made of my specifics leading up to the application. My small scale project fitted completely into "permitted development" save for its exact location relative to my dwellinghouse, chiefly because my (long) garden leads from the house towards the highway. So as a factor of just 10 metres of precise location, the project transforms from essentially "shed replacement" to "full-on ecological and biodiversity impact assessment" and from "DIY graph paper and pencil" to "Plans, location maps and supporting documentation." There appears to be little interim scaling of the system or indeed cost. Had I been able to place my small garden project just 10 metres further North (I cannot), I would have a) avoided the whole planning system, b) saved myself £347 and, importantly c) saved the planning department considerable time/effort.
